name: KSU ASCEND Program
summary: ASCEND supports KSU students with foster care or homelessness experience through campus-based retention help.
eligibility: KSU students with foster care or homelessness experience.
address: Kennesaw State University, Kennesaw and Marietta, Georgia

name: KSU CARE Services
summary: KSU CARE Services helps students facing housing, food, and emergency basic-needs barriers.
eligibility: KSU students needing basic-needs or short-term crisis support.
address: Kennesaw State University, Kennesaw and Marietta, Georgia

name: Embark Georgia Campus Programs
summary: Embark Georgia connects students with campus support programs, scholarships, and college persistence help.
eligibility: Georgia students with foster care or homelessness experience seeking postsecondary support.

name: Embark Georgia - Chafee ETV
summary: Embark Georgia administers Chafee ETV funding for Georgia youth with qualifying foster care history.
eligibility: Youth ages 18 to 25 with qualifying foster care history and education or training plans.

name: Connected By 21
summary: Connected By 21 organizes Georgia supports for youth aging out, including housing, life skills, and transition planning.
eligibility: Georgia youth transitioning from foster care who need post-18 support planning.

name: Georgia DFCS Youth Information
summary: Georgia DFCS Youth Information highlights transition resources, youth programs, and foster-care planning supports.
eligibility: Georgia youth in care or transitioning out of care.

name: Georgia CREW
summary: Georgia CREW helps youth in care build work readiness, career exposure, and employment skills.
eligibility: Georgia youth in foster care, generally ages 14 to 21, seeking workforce preparation.

name: SNAP Works for Former Foster Youth
summary: SNAP Works can connect eligible former foster youth to training, credentials, and employment support.
eligibility: Former foster youth who meet SNAP Works program criteria in participating areas.

name: Wellroot Family Services
summary: Wellroot supports youth transitioning from foster care with housing-focused and wraparound services.
eligibility: Transition-age youth needing housing support, especially youth leaving foster care.

name: 211 Georgia
summary: 211 Georgia connects people to local housing, food, crisis, health, and community services.
eligibility: Anyone in Georgia needing help finding community resources.

name: Georgia DFCS Emergency Support
summary: Georgia DFCS provides emergency child welfare and foster-care contact pathways for urgent family or youth safety needs.
eligibility: Georgia residents needing urgent child welfare or foster-care support.

name: Georgia Medicaid Extension for Former Foster Youth
summary: Georgia extends Medicaid for many youth who were in foster care at age 18.
eligibility: Youth who were in foster care at age 18 may qualify for health coverage through age 26.

name: Extended Youth Support Services (EYSS)
summary: EYSS is part of Georgia's extended foster-care support pathway for eligible youth who aged out at 18.
eligibility: Youth who aged out at 18 and meet Georgia extended foster-care participation rules.

name: Cobb Homeless Alliance
summary: Cobb Homeless Alliance coordinates housing providers and homelessness response resources in Cobb County.
eligibility: Cobb County residents experiencing homelessness or housing instability.

name: MUST Ministries
summary: MUST Ministries offers emergency assistance, shelter-linked support, food, and basic-needs help in metro Atlanta.
eligibility: People in crisis needing food, housing support, or basic-needs assistance.

name: Atlanta Legal Aid - Cobb County
summary: Atlanta Legal Aid's Cobb office helps eligible residents with housing, family, and public-benefits legal issues.
eligibility: Low-income Cobb County residents with qualifying civil legal needs.

name: Georgia Legal Services Program
summary: Georgia Legal Services Program provides civil legal help to eligible Georgians outside metro Atlanta coverage areas.
eligibility: Low-income Georgians with qualifying civil legal problems.

name: Georgia Gateway
summary: Georgia Gateway is the main online portal for Medicaid, SNAP, TANF, and other state-administered benefits.
eligibility: Georgia residents applying for public benefits or managing an existing benefits case.

name: Opening a Checking or Savings Account in Georgia
summary: To open a checking or savings account in Georgia you need a valid photo ID (Georgia state ID, driver's license, or US passport), your Social Security number or ITIN, and proof of current address such as a lease, utility bill, or mail from a government agency. Banks do not run a credit check to open a standard checking or savings account, so a limited or missing credit history does not block you — which is why these accounts are a good fit for foster youth, former foster youth, and other transition-age young adults. Credit unions such as Georgia's Own and Delta Community routinely open accounts for young adults and former foster youth with no credit history. Bank On certified accounts, available at many Georgia banks and credit unions, are designed for people who have been unbanked or underbanked; they have no overdraft fees and a low minimum opening deposit (often $25 or less), which makes them especially useful for youth leaving foster care. If you do not have a current address on file, a DFCS case manager or transitional housing program can help you document a mailing address.
eligibility: Anyone 18 or older with valid ID and a Social Security number or ITIN. Minors may open joint accounts with a trusted adult.

name: Georgia's Own Credit Union
summary: Georgia's Own is a member-owned credit union open to anyone who lives, works, worships, or attends school in Georgia. They offer student checking, basic checking, and savings accounts with no monthly fee for eligible accounts and no credit check required to open a standard account. Membership requires a $5 opening deposit into a savings account.
eligibility: Anyone who lives, works, worships, or attends school in Georgia. $5 minimum opens membership.

name: Replacing a Social Security Card in Georgia
summary: To replace a lost or stolen Social Security card, apply through the Social Security Administration (SSA). Replacement cards are free. You need one primary proof of identity — a U.S. driver's license, state-issued ID card, or U.S. passport is preferred. If you don't have any of those, SSA may accept two secondary documents such as an employer ID, school ID, health insurance card (not Medicare), or military ID. You also need to show proof of U.S. citizenship or lawful immigration status if that isn't already on file with SSA; a certified U.S. birth certificate works for citizenship. If you already have a my Social Security online account and a U.S. mailing address, you can usually request the replacement at ssa.gov without visiting an office. Otherwise, apply in person at any Georgia SSA field office. SSA limits replacements to three per year and ten in a lifetime, though replacements tied to a legal name change don't count against those limits. A new card typically arrives in the mail within 10 to 14 business days. If you don't have a photo ID, ask your DFCS case manager — they can often help you get one or write a verification letter the SSA will accept in place of some ID documents.
eligibility: Anyone with a Social Security number needing a replacement card. U.S. citizens and lawful residents.

name: Getting a Georgia State ID or Driver's License as a Foster Youth
summary: The Georgia Department of Driver Services (DDS) issues state ID cards and driver's licenses. Youth currently in foster care and former foster youth who meet DDS eligibility rules can qualify for a fee waiver, meaning the ID or license is issued at no cost. To apply, bring one primary proof of identity (certified U.S. birth certificate, U.S. passport, or permanent resident card), proof of your Social Security number (Social Security card, W-2, or 1099), and two proofs of Georgia residency (lease, utility bill, bank statement, school record, or a letter from a group home or placement provider). Foster youth applying for the fee waiver should also bring documentation of foster-care status — a verification letter from your DFCS case manager is the standard document, and DDS can confirm what they accept at your service center. Apply in person at any Georgia DDS Customer Service Center; most centers issue a temporary paper license the same day and mail the plastic card within 2 to 4 weeks. If you do not yet have a certified birth certificate, get one first — Georgia DPH Vital Records issues them, and a DFCS case manager can help request it on your behalf.
eligibility: Georgia residents. Fee waiver: youth currently in foster care or former foster youth meeting DDS eligibility criteria. Ask your DFCS case manager for a verification letter.

name: Getting a Georgia Birth Certificate
summary: Georgia birth certificates are issued by the Georgia Department of Public Health (DPH) State Office of Vital Records. A certified copy typically costs $25, with additional copies ordered at the same time at $5 each — fees are set by DPH and can change, so check the Vital Records website for current pricing before you go. You can order the certificate if you are the person named on the record (once you are 18 or older), a parent listed on the record, a spouse, adult child, sibling, grandparent, or a legal guardian with proof of relationship. For youth in foster care, a DFCS case manager has the authority to request a birth certificate on your behalf, and they do this routinely as part of Independent Living Program transition services when youth need vital records for school, benefits, or ID applications — so yes, a caseworker can pull it for you. You can order in person at the State Office of Vital Records in Atlanta or at any Georgia county vital records office (usually fastest, often same-day), by mail using the DPH vital records application form (4 to 6 weeks), or online through the authorized vendor VitalChek (7 to 10 business days, with additional vendor fees). If you were born outside Georgia, Georgia cannot issue another state's birth certificate — you must contact the vital records office of the state where you were born. The CDC's National Center for Health Statistics publishes a directory of every state's vital records office, and a DFCS case manager can help you navigate that. To order, you need a valid photo ID (state ID, driver's license, or U.S. passport). If you do not have a photo ID yet, a DFCS case manager can submit the request on your behalf or help you get an ID first.
eligibility: Person on the record (age 18+), immediate family with proof of relationship, legal guardian or custodian, or a DFCS case manager acting on behalf of a youth in care.
services: DFCS case manager assistance for youth in care
source_name: Georgia Department of Public Health Vital Records

name: Georgia Tuition and Fee Waiver for Foster Youth
summary: Georgia state law authorizes a Tuition and Fee Waiver program that waives tuition and mandatory fees at any University System of Georgia (USG) institution — including Kennesaw State, Georgia State, UGA, Georgia Tech, and every other USG college or university — and at any Technical College System of Georgia (TCSG) institution for eligible current and former foster youth. Typical eligibility: you were in Georgia foster care at age 14 or older, you were adopted from Georgia foster care at age 14 or older, or you left foster care through a permanency outcome after age 14. You generally must enroll by age 26, meet the school's regular admission requirements, and maintain Satisfactory Academic Progress. The waiver covers tuition and mandatory fees only — books, housing, food, and transportation are not covered, and most students stack Chafee ETV, Pell Grant, HOPE, Zell Miller, and other aid on top. Coverage typically runs up to a first bachelor's degree or five academic years, whichever comes first. To apply: (1) apply and get admitted to a Georgia public college or university, (2) complete the FAFSA, (3) contact that school's financial aid office to request the tuition waiver, and (4) provide documentation of your foster-care history — a verification letter from a DFCS case manager is the standard document. Embark Georgia campus coordinators at many Georgia public institutions help youth navigate the waiver and stack the rest of the aid package. Exact eligibility rules and documentation can shift year to year — confirm current rules with the Georgia Student Finance Commission (GSFC) and the financial aid office at your school.
eligibility: Current or former Georgia foster youth, typically with foster-care involvement at age 14 or older. Must enroll by age 26 at a public Georgia college or university. Must meet admission requirements and maintain Satisfactory Academic Progress. Confirm current rules with GSFC and your school's financial aid office.
services: tuition waiver at public Georgia colleges and universities
coordination with FAFSA, Pell Grant, and Chafee ETV

name: FAFSA for Foster Youth in Georgia
summary: The FAFSA (Free Application for Federal Student Aid) at studentaid.gov is the single form that unlocks federal Pell Grants, subsidized and unsubsidized student loans, work-study, and most Georgia state aid — including the HOPE Scholarship, Zell Miller, and the Georgia Tuition and Fee Waiver for foster youth. Foster youth get a major advantage: you file as an INDEPENDENT STUDENT, which means you do not list any parent information on the form — not your biological parents, not your foster parents, not an adoptive parent, not a guardian. When the FAFSA asks whether at any time since you turned 13 you were in foster care, a dependent or ward of the court, or both your parents were deceased, answer YES. That single answer makes you independent for federal aid purposes, and only your own income (typically low or zero for a transition-age youth) counts in the aid formula — which usually produces a full or near-full Pell Grant. The FAFSA opens each year for the following academic year — in recent years the form has opened in October or December, so check studentaid.gov for the current year's open date. Georgia priority deadlines are school-specific but often fall in winter or early spring, so file as soon as the form opens. To complete the FAFSA you need your Social Security number, a Federal Student Aid ID (FSA ID, created at studentaid.gov), and any tax or income records you have; most foster youth have little or no income to report. If you do not have all your documents yet, your DFCS case manager, an Embark Georgia campus coordinator, KSU ASCEND staff, or the financial aid office at your school can walk you through the form — do not try to do it alone if you hit a question you are unsure about.
eligibility: Anyone applying for federal or Georgia student aid. Foster youth qualify as independent students and do not list parent information.
services: Georgia HOPE and Zell Miller qualification
gateway to Georgia Tuition and Fee Waiver
source_name: U.S. Department of Education Federal Student Aid

name: HUD Foster Youth to Independence (FYI) Voucher
summary: The Foster Youth to Independence (FYI) voucher is a federal HUD program that provides rental assistance for youth leaving or aging out of foster care. FYI uses the Housing Choice Voucher framework — the federal government pays a portion of your rent in the private market while you work toward stable income. Eligibility: you must be at least 18 and under 25, you must have left foster care at age 16 or older (or be about to leave at 16+), and you must be homeless or at risk of homelessness. You cannot apply directly to HUD — FYI vouchers are referred to local Public Housing Authorities (PHAs) by the Public Child Welfare Agency, which in Georgia is DFCS. Start the process by telling your DFCS case manager or Independent Living Program specialist that you want an FYI referral; they will contact a participating Georgia PHA on your behalf. Vouchers typically last up to 36 months under current HUD rules and can be paired with self-sufficiency services like employment coaching. Not every Georgia PHA has an active FYI partnership at any given time — availability depends on local HUD, PHA, and DFCS agreements. Georgia PHAs that have historically participated include Atlanta Housing Authority, Marietta Housing Authority (Cobb), DeKalb Housing Authority, and several metro and mid-sized county PHAs. If the PHA in your county is not currently accepting FYI referrals, Connected By 21, Wellroot, and other DFCS-partnered transitional living programs can bridge you to stable housing while you wait.
eligibility: Age 18 to under 25. Left foster care at age 16 or older (or leaving at 16+). Homeless or at risk of homelessness. Must be referred by DFCS — youth cannot self-apply to HUD.
source_name: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development

name: FDIC Money Smart for Young People
summary: FDIC Money Smart for Young People is a free, plain-language curriculum with modules on earning, spending, saving, borrowing, and protecting money. It does not require an account or sign-up. Topics include how to build a monthly budget, how to track spending against income, how checking and savings accounts work, how to read a bank statement, and how to avoid common financial scams. It is the federal government's standard financial-literacy resource for teens and young adults.
eligibility: Free and open to anyone. No account required.

name: Covenant House Georgia
summary: 24/7 crisis shelter and transitional housing for youth experiencing homelessness or escaping trafficking.
eligibility: Youth ages 18-24; no cost; open 24/7; walk-ins accepted.

name: Lost-n-Found Youth
summary: LGBTQ+-focused shelter, transitional housing, and 24/7 youth support for young adults facing homelessness.
eligibility: LGBTQ+ youth ages 18-25; metro Atlanta; 24/7 hotline.

name: CHRIS 180 — The SPOT Drop-In Center
summary: Stigma-free drop-in center where transition-age youth build life skills, get tutoring, GED prep, and counseling.
eligibility: Youth ages 16-26; walk-in; no cost; Mon-Fri 9am-5pm.

name: WorkSource Atlanta — Youth R.I.S.E.
summary: WIOA-funded youth workforce program offering paid work experience, GED support, and career coaching at no cost.
eligibility: Ages 16-24; City of Atlanta residents; foster-care status is a qualifying WIOA barrier.

name: Atlanta Legal Aid Society — Fulton Office
summary: Free civil legal help for low-income residents, including foster-youth advocacy, housing, benefits, and ID issues.
eligibility: Low-income Fulton residents; civil matters only; includes current and former foster youth.

name: Latin American Association — Gwinnett Outreach Center
summary: Lead agency for Gwinnett's Coordinated Entry System, connecting youth and families to emergency shelter, transitional housing, and rapid rehousing.
eligibility: Any Gwinnett resident in housing crisis; dedicated line for parenting youth ages 18-24; immigrant-friendly; English and Spanish.

name: Good Samaritan Health Center of Gwinnett
summary: Low-cost primary medical and dental care for uninsured Gwinnett residents, with Spanish-language support.
eligibility: Uninsured or under-insured Gwinnett residents of any age; teens welcome; bilingual staff available.
source_name: Good Samaritan Health Center of Gwinnett

name: View Point Health — Gwinnett
summary: Gwinnett's community behavioral health center offering therapy, psychiatric care, and adolescent crisis stabilization.
eligibility: Open to Gwinnett, Rockdale, and Newton residents including adolescents; Medicaid, private insurance, and sliding-scale.

name: Gwinnett County CASA
summary: Court-appointed volunteer advocates for abused and neglected children in Gwinnett Juvenile Court dependency cases.
eligibility: Children and youth in Gwinnett foster care or juvenile court dependency; no cost.

name: Gwinnett County WIOA One-Stop Career Resource Center
summary: Free job training, career advising, and WIOA-funded skills programs for Gwinnett youth and adults.
eligibility: Income-eligible older youth ages 18-24, laid-off workers, and adults seeking skills; Gwinnett residency.
address: 3805 Crestwood Pkwy NW Suite 300, Duluth, GA 30096

name: Clayton County CASA
summary: Court-appointed volunteer advocates representing the best interests of abused and neglected children in Clayton Juvenile Court.
eligibility: Children and youth 0-21 with an active dependency case in Clayton Juvenile Court; court referral.

name: Clayton Center — Child & Young Adult Services
summary: County behavioral-health authority providing mental-health care and 24/7 crisis support for children, teens, and young adults.
eligibility: Ages 4-21 (if still in high school); foster parents and guardians welcome; Medicaid, PeachCare, sliding-scale.

name: The D.O.R.M. (Development of Responsible Men)
summary: Transitional housing in Riverdale for young men aging out of foster care, offering up to 24 months of supported independent living.
eligibility: Males ages 18-24 who aged out of foster care, exited juvenile detention, or are in housing crisis.

name: WorkSource Atlanta Regional NextGen Youth — Clayton
summary: WIOA-funded year-round workforce program offering paid work experience and occupational training for Clayton youth.
eligibility: Ages 16-24, Clayton County resident, meet low-income or barrier criteria (foster care qualifies automatically).
address: 3000 Corporate Center Dr Suite 350, Morrow, GA 30260

name: Clayton County Public Schools — Children in Transition
summary: District McKinney-Vento program ensuring homeless and foster-placed students stay enrolled with wraparound school support.
eligibility: Clayton students (PK-12) who are homeless, in transition, or awaiting foster placement per McKinney-Vento definitions.
address: 2260 Old Rex Morrow Rd, Ellenwood, GA 30294

name: Park Place Outreach Youth Emergency Shelter
summary: 12-bed emergency shelter and transitional living program for runaway, homeless, and at-risk youth in Savannah.
eligibility: Youth ages 11-17 for emergency shelter; transitional living program serves older adolescent males at risk of homelessness.

name: J.C. Lewis Primary Health Care Center — Pediatric Center
summary: Federally Qualified Health Center pediatric site offering primary care, immunizations, and behavioral health on a sliding fee scale.
eligibility: Pediatric through adolescent patients; sliding-scale fees, Medicaid and uninsured accepted; no residency requirement.

name: Savannah CASA (Brightside Child & Family Advocacy)
summary: Court-appointed volunteer advocates for Chatham County foster youth, plus Brighter Futures mentoring for youth aging out.
eligibility: Children and youth birth through 21 in Chatham juvenile court dependency cases; Brighter Futures serves older foster youth.

name: WorkSource Coastal Georgia — WIOA Youth Program
summary: Federally funded workforce program offering paid work experience, training, and education support for low-income youth in the coastal region.
eligibility: Youth ages 16-24, in-school or out-of-school, low-income or facing barriers (foster-care status qualifies); Chatham + 9 other coastal counties.
address: 1000 Business Center Drive Suite 100, Savannah, GA 31405

name: Goodwill Southeast Georgia — Savannah Opportunity Center
summary: Free career center offering digital skills training, tuition-free certifications, and job placement support.
eligibility: Open to anyone seeking career services; mentorship track for at-risk youth; walk-ins welcome.

name: Goshen New Beginnings
summary: Independent living program providing housing, life coaching, and career support for youth aging out of foster care.
eligibility: Foster youth ages 17-21.

name: MUST Ministries — Cherokee Client Services
summary: Wraparound assistance for Cherokee residents in crisis, including food, clothing, housing help, and workforce development.
eligibility: Cherokee County residents in need; no age minimum and no religious requirement.

name: CASA of Cherokee County (The Children's Haven)
summary: Court-appointed volunteer advocates speaking for children in foster care throughout dependency cases.
eligibility: Children and youth in Cherokee County Juvenile Court dependency cases.
source_name: The Children's Haven of Cherokee County

name: Georgia Highlands Medical Services — Canton Family Health Center
summary: Federally Qualified Health Center offering primary care on a sliding-fee scale regardless of insurance status.
eligibility: Open to all ages including uninsured; sliding fee based on income.

name: Cherokee Family Violence Center
summary: 24/7 domestic violence crisis response with emergency shelter, counseling, and legal advocacy for Cherokee County.
eligibility: Survivors of domestic violence including teens and young adults; dedicated teen program.
